one_line_takeaway: AGENTARMOR reframes an LLM agent's runtime trace as a program, builds a Program Dependence Graph with LLM-inferred data/control dependencies plus integrity/confidentiality typing, and blocks injected tool calls before execution — reaching 3% ASR on AgentDojo and ~0% on ASB at only 1% utility loss, at the cost of ~3x runtime and ~13.6k extra tokens.
key_finding: On AgentDojo with GPT-4o-mini, AGENTARMOR cuts average ASR from a 17% no-defense baseline to 3% while losing only 1% utility (72% vs 73% baseline); on ASB it drives ASR to ~0% (from 41% GPT-4o-mini / 73% GPT-4o baselines) with TPR 1.00 and near-zero FPR. It matches or beats prompt-level and system-level baselines while preserving far more utility than the utility-costly ones. The trade-off is runtime overhead: 20.89s per task vs a 6.17s no-defense base (still cheaper than Camel's 46.72s), dominated (~69%) by graph construction, and ~13,609 extra tokens.
core_contribution: AGENTARMOR is a runtime guardrail that defends LLM agents against prompt injection by treating the agent's execution trace as a program and applying classic program-analysis techniques to it. Its own novelty (distinct from the cited Program Dependence Graph of Ferrante et al. 1987, which it reuses) is three-fold: (1) the paradigm of abstracting an agent's runtime trace (system/user prompts, thoughts, tool calls, observations) into a Program Dependence Graph (PDG) built from a control-flow graph plus data-flow graph, so that control and data dependencies of each tool call become explicit; (2) a 'dependency analyzer' that infers those dependencies from natural-language reasoning by prompting an LLM (gpt-4o-mini) to match the trace against 8 formalized 'reasoning patterns' (e.g., Direct User Request, Parameterized Execution, Transfer Execution, Unauthorized Indirect Execution); and (3) an enforcement layer comprising a graph annotator that assigns confidentiality/integrity security types (low/mid/high) plus rule types over a security lattice, and a graph inspector that traverses the annotated PDG and blocks any tool call violating an integrity/confidentiality constraint before it executes. The distinguishing pitch versus cited access-control defenses (Progent, Camel) is fine-grained per-parameter dependency tracking rather than treating a tool action as a coarse whole.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against tool-using LLM agents. The attacker is an external party who cannot talk to the agent directly but controls content the agent is instructed to process (emails, webpages, files), and injects instructions there to induce unintended tool actions; the attacker knows the agent's exposed tools and general reasoning structure and can attack over multiple rounds (including cross-resource 'two-hop' write-then-read pollution, e.g., a malicious command saved to a file later executed). The defender is the system operator who controls the agent's architecture and planning loop and can instrument/inspect thoughts, tool selections, and parameter values before each tool execution, but does not control user inputs or processed content. Explicitly out of scope: compromised/lying tool binaries and malicious backends, model-level attacks (backdoor/poisoning), and DoS (the current design has no 'end' action to reason about termination). The paper frames three root-cause challenges: untraceable data dependencies, untraceable control dependencies, and cross-resource data-flow ambiguity.
limitations: Core enforcement decisions (dependency inference) rely on another prompted LLM, so the analyzer is itself potentially bypassable and adds latency/token cost. It cannot handle DoS attacks because the model lacks an 'end' action. Rule types are mostly predefined/static, limiting adaptivity to new tools/tasks. A residual failure mode ('transfer execution'): when the user explicitly tells the agent to read a file and 'follow the instructions precisely', injected content in that file is treated as high-integrity and executed — the disallow setting lowers ASR/raises TPR but hurts utility/FPR because many benign traces legitimately need this pattern. Utility varies strongly by backbone (Gemini-2.0 41.7%, Llama-3.3-70B 58.1% vs Claude-3-7 87%), so the defense's utility preservation is model-dependent. ASB coverage is narrowed to observation prompt injection only. Camel achieved 0% ASR (marginally better defense) because its information-flow control over generated code is not LLM-dependent, whereas AGENTARMOR's analysis is.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (benchmark; domains Banking, Slack, Travel, Workspace)
ASB (Agent Security Bench; only the observation prompt injection / OPI setting used)
quantitative_results: AgentDojo overall ASR reduced to 3% (from 17% no-defense baseline) with GPT-4o-mini
AgentDojo utility 72% under AGENTARMOR vs 73% no-defense baseline (1% loss)
GPT-4o on AgentDojo: ASR 4% (down from 28% baseline)
ASB overall ASR reduced to ~0% (from 41% GPT-4o-mini / 73% GPT-4o baselines); TPR 1.00, FPR ~0.00-0.02

Per-model ASR/utility (model ablation, Fig.9; gpt-4o-mini figures here differ slightly from the §6.2 overall 3%/72% because this is the separate ablation run): Claude-3-7-sonnet 0.03% ASR / 87% utility; gpt-4o-mini 2% ASR / 76.3% utility; gpt-4o 5.2% ASR / 71.8% utility; Gemini-2.0-flash 0.3% ASR / 41.7% utility; Llama-3.3-70B 0.8% ASR / 58.1% utility
Runtime overhead: 20.89s per task vs 6.17s no-defense base (Camel 46.72s, Progent 11.24s); graph constructor ~69% of time, annotator+inspector 5.4% (1.13s)
Token overhead: AGENTARMOR ~13,609 tokens (72% of total token cost)
baselines_compared: AgentDojo prompt-level: repeat user prompt (11% ASR), spotlighting with delimiting (14% ASR), tool filter (3% ASR, small utility loss, ~0.68 utility vs 0.73 baseline), transformers PI detector (8% ASR but ~30% avg utility drop, ~0.43 utility, due to high FPR)
ASB prompt-level: delimiters, sandwich prevention, instructional prevention (all >30% ASR)
Finetuning-level: SecAlign-70B (2% ASR / 76% utility; needs model fine-tuning)
System-level access control: Progent (2% ASR / 64% utility), Camel (0% ASR / 48% utility)
